title: SSRF via WebSocket upgrade proxying in Next.js self-hosted router-server
description: Next.js's built-in Node.js router server handles HTTP `upgrade` (WebSocket) requests in router-server.ts. The upgrade handler runs resolveRoutes() with isUpgradeReq:true and then, if no internal output matched, proxies the request to parsedUrl whenever parsedUrl.protocol is set: `if (parsedUrl.protocol) { return await proxyRequest(req, socket, parsedUrl, head) }`. Unlike the normal HTTP request path, the upgrade path does not check that routing actually finished and explicitly marked the request as a safe external rewrite. An attacker who can reach the self-hosted origin server can therefore send a crafted WebSocket upgrade request whose resolved URL points at an arbitrary internal or external destination, causing the server to open a proxied connection to that host (e.g. internal services or cloud metadata endpoints). Vercel-hosted deployments are not affected.
